chunks.title: Catalog Description
chunks.content: Data Mining and Analysis. (3-0). Credit 3. Broad overview of data mining, integrating related concepts from machine learning and statistics; exploratory data analysis, pattern mining, clustering and classification; applications to scientific and online data. Cross-listed with ECEN 758 and STAT 639.

chunks.title: Course Prerequisites
chunks.content: I expect all students to have had some previous exposure to basic probability, statistics, algorithms, and data structures. You should be able to design and develop large programs and learn new software libraries on your own.

chunks.title: Course Learning Outcomes
chunks.content:
Upon completion of this course, the learner will be able to:
• Define and explain the key concepts and models relevant to data mining and analysis, including data preparation, normalization, dimensionality reduction, text mining, and so on.
• Design, implement, and evaluate data mining algorithms like associate rules, clustering, anomaly detection, and do so on modern scalable cloud computing platforms (e.g., Spark).
• Identify the salient features and apply recent research results in data mining, including topics such as graph mining, and large-scale mining.

chunks.title: Textbook and/or Resource Materials
chunks.content:
This material Is: Required
Course readings will be drawn from a variety of online textbooks, scholarly papers, and other resources. One important textbook is listed below.

This material Is: Required
Mining of Massive Data Sets
ISBN: 9781108476348
Authors: Jure Leskovec, Anand Rajaraman, Jeffrey David Ullman
Publisher: Cambridge University Press
Publication Date: 2020-01-09

chunks.title: Grading Policy
chunks.content:
10% Weekly Assessments
50% Homework
40% Individual Semester-Long Project

A >= 90, B >= 80 and < 90, C >= 70 and < 80, D >= 60 and < 70, F < 60

Weekly Assessments: Each week will have one or more short quizzes (typically true/false, multiple choice) over that week's material. These assessments may be taken multiple times, with only the highest score kept for grading.

Homework: We will have homework assignments every week. For the first twelve weeks, these will be worth 4% of your grade. The 13th homework will be worth 2% of your grade. All homework assignments will be Python notebooks, with a mix of programming-based problems, written problems, and other questions (e.g., post a video in response to a particular query, post a response on a Canvas Discussion).

chunks.title: AI-coding assistant requirement
chunks.content: We also require all students to use an AI-coding assistant (e.g., Cursor, Cline, CoPilot, etc.). The future of coding (imho) is 5x or 10x developers who can smartly use AI to accelerate their productivity. So let's get started practicing now. We make no expectations that you have been exposed to Python before, but we do expect you to come up to speed rapidly.

chunks.title: Homework Collaboration Clarification
chunks.content:
All homework assignments must be submitted by 11:59pm Central time on the due date. For the homework assignments, you may talk to any other class member or work in groups to discuss the problems in a general way. However, your actual detailed solution must be yours alone. If you do talk to other students, you must write on your assignment who it is that you discussed the problems with. Your submitted work must be written solely by you and not contain work directly copied from others.

To clarify, your homework is yours alone and you are expected to complete each homework independently. Your solution should be written by you without the direct aid or help of anyone else. However, we believe that collaboration and team work are important for facilitating learning, so we encourage you to discuss problems and general problem approaches (but not actual solutions) with your classmates. If you do have a chat with another student about a homework problem, you must inform us by writing a note on your homework submission (e.g., Bob pointed me to the relevant section for problem 3). The basic rule is that no student should explicitly share a solution with another student (and thereby circumvent the basic learning process), but it is okay to share general approaches, directions, and so on. If you feel like you have an issue that needs clarification, feel free to contact either me or the TA.

chunks.title: Individual Semester-Long Project
chunks.content:
Over the course of the semester, you will work on a significant data mining project. The project is broken into five major deliverables:
1. Data Selection (Feb 3)
2. Initial Research Question (Mar 5)
3. Deep Dive (Apr 2)
4. Project Showcase (week of Apr 20)
5. Final Deliverable (Apr 27)

chunks.title: Late Work Policy
chunks.content: Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy (Student Rule 7).

chunks.title: Course Specific Late Work Policy
chunks.content:
Late homework assignments will be penalized 1% per hour after the deadline, up to a maximum of 48 hours. After 48 hours, the assignment will receive a 0. To illustrate, an assignment submitted at 5:05am will be penalized 6 points (so it will be graded out of 94, instead of 100).

For the Individual Semester-Long Project, students will have FIVE late days in total for the first three deliverables -- Data Selection (Feb 3), Initial Research Question (Mar 5), and Deep Dive (Apr 2). The final two deliverables -- Project Showcase (week of Apr 20) and Final Deliverable (Apr 27) -- will not be accepted late.

chunks.title: Course Schedule
chunks.content:
Week 1 :: Course introduction
Week 2 :: Frequent Itemset Mining
Week 3 :: Graph Mining
Week 4 :: Graph Mining and Large-Scale ML
Week 5 :: Large-Scale ML
Week 6 :: Clustering
Week 7 :: Text Mining
Week 8 :: Data Mining and Distributed Computing
Week 9 :: Anomaly Detection
Week 10 :: Graph Embeddings
Week 11 :: Locality-Sensitive Hashing
Week 12 :: Stream Mining
Week 13 :: Hot Topics in Data Mining
Week 14 :: Project Showcase
Week 15 :: Finalize Project Deliverables

chunks.title: Major Project Deadlines
chunks.content:
1. Data Selection (Feb 3)
2. Initial Research Question (Mar 5)
3. Deep Dive (Apr 2)
4. Project Showcase (week of Apr 20)
5. Final Deliverable (Apr 27)
